text: The Code may be cited as the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. It extends to the whole of Pakistan and applies to proceedings in all Civil Courts.
Definitions for terms such as 'decree', 'order', 'judgment', 'foreign court', 'legal representative', 'mesne profits', etc.
Subordination of Courts defined. District Court is subject to High Court and subordinate Civil Courts are subject to District Court.
No proceedings in Civil Courts to be stayed unless expressly provided.
Saving of powers of certain Revenue Officers as Civil Courts.
Courts shall not try suits of a civil nature when barred by express or implied provisions of law.
Provides detailed enumeration of courts’ jurisdiction on valuation of suits.
Presidency Small Cause Courts Act not affected by this Code.
Courts shall have jurisdiction to try all suits of a civil nature except those expressly or impliedly barred.
No court shall proceed with trial of a suit in which the matter is directly and substantially in issue in a previously instituted suit.
No court shall try any suit in which the matter has been directly and substantially in issue in a former suit between the same parties (res judicata).
Bar to further suit where plaintiff is precluded by rules from instituting another suit.
A foreign judgment shall be conclusive between the parties except in certain cases such as fraud or lack of jurisdiction.
Court shall presume, upon production of a certified copy, that a foreign judgment was pronounced by a competent court.
Every suit shall be instituted in the court of the lowest grade competent to try it.
Suits to be instituted where the subject matter is situated (immovable property).
Where a suit is for immovable property situated in different jurisdictions, it may be instituted in any court within whose jurisdiction any portion lies.
Where local limits of jurisdiction of courts are uncertain, High Court may decide the jurisdiction.
Suits for compensation for wrongs to person or movables may be instituted where wrong was committed or defendant resides.
Other suits to be instituted where defendant resides, carries on business, or where cause of action arises.
Objection as to jurisdiction must be raised at the earliest possible opportunity.
Where a suit may be instituted in more than one court, the defendant may apply for transfer of the suit.
The court to which an application under Section 22 is made shall decide the application after notice to the other party.
High Court or District Court may transfer or withdraw any suit, appeal or other proceeding.
Supreme Court may transfer any suit or proceeding from one High Court to another.
Every suit shall be instituted by presenting a plaint in duplicate.
Summons to the defendant shall be issued to appear and answer the claim.
Service of summons where defendant resides in another province.
Service of foreign summonses shall be governed by the law in force in the foreign country.
Court may order discovery, inspection, attendance, production of documents or facts.
Summons to witness may be issued by the court on application by the parties.
Court may compel attendance of witnesses by issuing warrant, fine or order for attachment of property.
Court shall pronounce judgment and on such judgment a decree shall follow.
Court may order interest to be paid on the principal amount adjudged from the date of suit to date of decree.
Costs of suit shall be in the discretion of the Court.
Compensatory costs may be awarded for false or vexatious claims or defenses.
Costs for causing delay may be imposed on party responsible for adjournments.
Provisions relating to execution of decrees and orders apply to all civil courts.
Court which passed a decree includes successor court or any court to which decree has been transferred.
A decree may be executed either by the court which passed it or by the court to which it is sent for execution.
Court may send decree for execution to another court of competent jurisdiction.
Transfer of decree to foreign court for execution shall be subject to conditions.
Result of execution to be certified to the court which passed the decree.
Court executing a decree has the same powers as the court which passed it.
Execution of decrees passed by civil courts in places to which the Code does not apply.
Execution of decrees passed by Revenue Courts.
Execution of decrees passed by courts in reciprocating territory may be executed as if passed by District Court.
Execution of decrees passed outside Pakistan by established civil courts.
Precept: Court which passed the decree may issue precept to another court to attach property.
All questions arising between parties relating to execution, discharge or satisfaction shall be determined by executing court.
Execution barred if not applied for within six years from date of decree.
Legal representative liable for execution of decree against deceased judgment-debtor.
Execution may be enforced against legal representative of a deceased judgment-debtor.
The court may execute a decree by delivery of property, attachment and sale, arrest and detention in prison, or appointment of a receiver, as deemed appropriate.
Decree for payment of money may be executed against any property of the judgment-debtor not exempted by law.
A decree against a legal representative may be executed only against the property of the deceased received by such legal representative.
Partition decrees shall be executed as prescribed by the Revenue authorities, when concerning agricultural land.
A judgment-debtor may be arrested and detained in civil prison in execution of a money decree, subject to conditions.
No woman shall be arrested or detained in execution of a decree for payment of money.
Subordinate courts must apply for arrest and detention to the District Court.
A judgment-debtor may be detained up to three months for amounts exceeding fifty rupees; up to six weeks if lesser.
Judgment-debtor released under Section 58 shall not be re-arrested in execution of the same decree.
Certain property of the judgment-debtor is exempt from attachment, including necessary wearing apparel, tools of artisans, and wages.
The Provincial Government may declare further exemptions from attachment via notification.
Private dwellings are protected against entry during certain hours for execution purposes.
In case of conflicting claims by multiple courts on the same property, the court of highest grade shall determine priority.
No private transfer of attached property shall affect the rights of the attaching creditor.
Purchaser of property sold in execution shall be deemed to have acquired the right, title, and interest of the judgment-debtor.
No suit shall be maintained against a purchaser on the ground that the purchase was made on behalf of another person.
The court shall determine the share of the judgment-debtor in attached property and proceed accordingly.
Provisions regarding execution of decrees are subject to rules in the First Schedule.
Obsolete; previously dealt with the powers of courts in princely states.
Decrees passed by courts in princely states prior to merger may be executed as if passed by a district court.
The executing court may direct removal of obstruction or resistance to possession by any person bound by the decree.
Resistance by a person claiming independent title may be adjudicated by the court.
When multiple decree-holders attach the same property, proceeds of sale shall be rateably distributed among them.
Orders under Sections 73 and others may be appealed under the Code.
The court has power to issue commissions to examine witnesses, make local investigations, and perform other inquiries.
A commission may be issued to another court for the purpose of recording evidence or other purposes.
Commission issued shall have the powers of a civil court in the execution of its duties.
Commissions may be issued to foreign tribunals, subject to rules and agreements.
In suits by or against the government, the government shall be named as the plaintiff or defendant, respectively.
No suit shall be instituted against the government or public officers unless notice of two months is given.
Public officers sued in their official capacity shall not be liable to arrest nor their property to attachment.
Execution of decree against the government shall not be issued without satisfaction being recorded or time lapse of three months.
Aliens may sue in courts in Pakistan, unless barred by treaty or law.
Religious institutions may sue or be sued in the name of their representative or manager.
A lunatic may sue or be sued through his legal guardian or next friend.
No foreign state may be sued without consent of the Federal Government certified in writing.
Suits by foreign states shall be in the name of the sovereign or head of the state.
Rulers of former Indian states may sue or be sued in the name of their sovereign title.
Interpleader suits may be instituted when multiple parties claim the same debt or property.
Courts may refer matters to arbitration, mediation, or conciliation with consent of parties.
The court may order public charities to be managed or directed under special circumstances.
Public nuisances and other wrongful acts affecting public rights may be the subject of suit by the Advocate-General or two or more persons.
Suits relating to breach of trust for public charities may be instituted by the Advocate-General or two or more persons with leave of the court.
The provisions of Sections 91 and 92 apply to Advocate-General of Pakistan or other authorized persons.
Court may issue interim orders to prevent ends of justice from being defeated including arrest before judgment and temporary injunctions.
Compensation may be awarded for arrest, attachment, or injunction obtained on insufficient grounds.
An appeal shall lie from every original decree to the Court authorized by law, unless otherwise barred.
Where a party could appeal from a preliminary decree but did not, they are barred from disputing its correctness in any appeal from the final decree.
When a Bench differs in opinion, the majority shall decide the appeal.
No decree shall be reversed or modified for immaterial error or irregularity not affecting the merits.
No order under Section 47 or Rule 72 of Order XXI shall be reversed unless it has caused failure of justice.
An appeal may lie to the High Court from every decree passed in appeal by a subordinate court if it involves a substantial question of law.
No further appeal shall lie from a decree passed in second appeal, except as provided under the Constitution.
No second appeal shall lie in suits of a nature cognizable by courts of small causes, when the subject matter does not exceed the prescribed value.
High Court may determine the issues of fact necessary for disposing of the appeal where evidence is sufficient.
Orders from which appeal lies include those under rules relating to injunctions, stays, etc., but not from any order passed in appeal.
No appeal lies from preliminary order unless it affects the decision of the case; errors may be challenged in appeal from final decree.
Where an order is appealable, the appeal shall lie to the court to which an appeal would lie from the decree.
Appellate court shall have same powers as the court of original jurisdiction including taking evidence, determining issues, and remanding.
Procedure for appeals from appellate decrees, and provisions applicable to such appeals.
Appeal lies to the Supreme Court from any judgment, decree or final order in civil proceedings if High Court certifies it involves a substantial question of law.
Appeal to Supreme Court may lie only if subject matter value exceeds the prescribed limit, unless question of law is involved.
No appeal shall lie from any judgment or order of a court passed with the consent of parties.
Savings for special jurisdiction and powers of High Courts under constitutional provisions.
Reference to High Court may be made by subordinate courts in case of doubt over legality or constitutionality of a rule or law.
Review may be sought in cases where no appeal lies, or on account of mistake or discovery of new evidence.
High Court may call for records from subordinate courts in cases of jurisdictional error or material irregularity.
High Court’s powers of superintendence over subordinate civil courts preserved.
Application of the Code to High Courts with original civil jurisdiction subject to modifications.
High Court may frame rules consistent with the Code for regulating its procedure.
Special provisions relating to Chartered High Courts preserved.
Sections 16 to 20 (relating to jurisdiction) do not apply to High Courts in their original civil jurisdiction.
Rules in the First Schedule have the effect of statutory law unless inconsistent with express Code provisions.
High Courts may amend rules in the First Schedule for their own jurisdiction.
Rule committees may be constituted for framing rules of procedure for civil courts.
Committee’s recommendations shall be submitted to High Court for approval.
After approval, rules shall be published in the official Gazette and shall have the force of law.
Rules may be made for service of summonses, pleadings, execution, and all matters of procedure.
Provisions apply to Presidency Small Cause Courts unless inconsistent with special local laws.
Rules may be framed to regulate the procedure of civil courts not being High Courts.
High Court may regulate its own procedure in original civil jurisdiction in the absence of contrary Code provisions.
Code shall not override local or special laws unless expressly stated.
Provisions relating to execution of decrees apply to orders passed under special laws, unless otherwise provided.
Women who, according to custom, do not appear in public shall be exempt from personal appearance in court.
Certain persons such as Judges and public officers shall be exempt from personal appearance in court.
Arrest or detention of persons exempt under Section 133 is prohibited.
Certain classes of persons are exempt from arrest under civil process in specific circumstances.
Exemption from arrest or detention applies to women in execution of money decrees.
Procedure for arrest or attachment outside the jurisdiction of the executing court.
Language of subordinate civil courts shall be as prescribed by Provincial Government.
Translation of judgments, decrees, and orders shall be provided when passed in a different language.
Oaths may be administered by the presiding judge of any civil court or authorized officer.
Officers of court may administer oaths and affirmations in proceedings under the Code.
Rules of procedure shall apply to all civil proceedings in any court.
Orders and notices under this Code to be in writing and signed by the Judge or officer.
Court may record reasons for adjournments granted during trial.
Court may order restitution when decree is reversed or modified and party has received benefits thereunder.
Sureties to a decree are liable to the extent of their engagement and may be proceeded against directly.
Proceedings may be taken by or against persons claiming under the parties to the suit.
Consent or agreement of a party may bind those who claim under them.
Court may enlarge the time fixed for doing an act even after expiration of the fixed time.
Right to lodge a caveat in any proceeding instituted or to be instituted in a suit.
Court may permit payment of deficient court-fees at any stage of the proceeding.
When jurisdiction is transferred, the transferee court may deal with the matter as if originally filed before it.
Court has inherent powers to make orders necessary for ends of justice or to prevent abuse of process.
Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in decrees or orders may be corrected by the court.
Court may amend any defect or error in proceedings at any time for determining the real question in controversy.
A party obtaining a judgment or order by fraud shall be liable to have it set aside.
Plaint and proceedings shall not be rejected merely due to technical errors if substantial compliance exists.
Savings of present rules: all rules made and in force continue to be valid unless inconsistent.
Provisions of the Limitation Act apply to applications made under the Code.
Rules made under the Code are subject to the provisions of the Code itself.
Provisions of the Code shall not affect powers conferred by any other law.
References in the Code to any enactment shall be construed as referring to the enactment in force in Pakistan.
Repealed; dealt with prior arrangements and savings when the Code was introduced.
Repealed; formerly related to suits by or against military personnel.
Repealed; covered procedure in certain regions now obsolete.
Repealed; earlier concerned the Presidency towns now defunct in current Pakistan law.
Repealed; relates to savings and transitional provisions during legislative updates.
Repealed; residual clauses, now subsumed under interpretation laws or removed.
